questions.questionText: In von Gierke disease, the mechanism causing hyperlipidemia along with hypoglycemia involves:
questions.optionB: Accumulated glucose-6-phosphate is shunted to glycolysis → excess acetyl-CoA → increased de novo lipogenesis AND increased VLDL secretion; additionally elevated glycerol-3-phosphate promotes TG synthesis
questions.optionD: Increased fat mobilization from adipose only
questions.explanation: Von Gierke (GSD type Ia, G6Pase deficiency) — metabolic cascade: G6P cannot be dephosphorylated → trapped in liver. Consequences: (1) HYPOGLYCEMIA (no free glucose release from liver), (2) LACTIC ACIDOSIS (excess G6P enters glycolysis → excess pyruvate → lactate), (3) HYPERLIPIDEMIA: excess acetyl-CoA from glycolysis drives de novo LIPOGENESIS; G3P from DHAP promotes TG esterification; increased VLDL secretion. (4) HYPERURICEMIA: shunting through HMP shunt → increased ribose-5-P → PRPP → purine synthesis → uric acid; also lactate competes with urate for renal excretion. Hepatomegaly from glycogen + fat accumulation. Reference: Harper's — Von Gierke.

questions.questionText: The net ATP yield from one molecule of glucose via aerobic glycolysis followed by complete oxidation is:
questions.optionB: 30-32 ATP (via malate-aspartate shuttle) or 28-30 ATP (glycerol-3-phosphate shuttle)
questions.explanation: Complete oxidation of 1 glucose (aerobic): Glycolysis: 2 ATP (substrate level) + 2 NADH. PDH: 2 NADH. TCA cycle: 6 NADH + 2 FADH2 + 2 GTP. Each NADH = 2.5 ATP, FADH2 = 1.5 ATP (ETC). Total: 10 NADH (25 ATP) + 2 FADH2 (3 ATP) + 2 ATP + 2 GTP = ~30-32 ATP. Malate-aspartate shuttle (heart/liver): cytoplasmic NADH → mitochondrial NADH = 2.5 ATP. Glycerol-3-P shuttle (muscle/brain): cytoplasmic NADH → FADH2 = 1.5 ATP. Reference: Harper's — ATP Yield.

questions.questionText: Pyruvate dehydrogenase complex (PDH) converts pyruvate to:
questions.optionB: Acetyl-CoA + CO2 + NADH
questions.explanation: PDH: irreversible oxidative decarboxylation connecting glycolysis to TCA cycle. Pyruvate + CoA + NAD+ → Acetyl-CoA + CO2 + NADH. Requires 5 coenzymes: TPP (B1), lipoic acid, CoA (pantothenic acid/B5), FAD (B2), NAD+ (niacin/B3). Activated by: insulin, Ca2+, pyruvate. Inhibited by: acetyl-CoA, NADH, ATP. Arsenic poisoning inhibits PDH (binds lipoic acid). Reference: Harper's — PDH Complex.

questions.questionText: The Cori cycle involves:
questions.optionB: Lactate from muscle → liver (gluconeogenesis → glucose) → blood → muscle
questions.explanation: Cori cycle: during anaerobic exercise, muscle produces lactate (from pyruvate, by LDH) → lactate enters blood → liver takes up lactate → reconverts to glucose via gluconeogenesis → glucose returns to blood → muscle. Net cost: 6 ATP consumed in liver, 2 ATP generated in muscle. Shifts metabolic burden from muscle to liver. Glucose-alanine cycle: similar but uses alanine instead of lactate (alanine carries NH2 group to liver for urea synthesis). Reference: Harper's — Cori Cycle.

questions.questionText: The oxidative phase of HMP shunt is irreversible and generates NADPH. In erythrocytes, complete deficiency of G6PD is lethal because:
questions.optionB: RBCs lack mitochondria and depend ENTIRELY on HMP shunt NADPH to maintain reduced glutathione (GSH) — without GSH, oxidative damage to hemoglobin and membrane lipids causes hemolysis and is incompatible with life
questions.optionC: RBCs cannot perform glycolysis without G6PD
questions.optionD: G6PD is needed for ATP production
questions.explanation: G6PD and RBC survival: RBCs are UNIQUELY vulnerable to oxidative stress because: (1) No mitochondria → no NADPH from NADP+-dependent isocitrate dehydrogenase, malic enzyme, etc., (2) Carry O2 → constant ROS exposure, (3) SOLE source of NADPH = HMP shunt (G6PD is rate-limiting). NADPH → glutathione reductase → reduces GSSG → GSH (reduced glutathione). GSH → glutathione peroxidase → detoxifies H2O2 and lipid peroxides. Complete G6PD absence = embryonic lethal. Partial deficiency (hemizygous males, X-linked): baseline function adequate but OXIDATIVE STRESS (fava beans, infection, drugs like primaquine/dapsone/sulfonamides) overwhelms residual capacity → Heinz bodies (denatured Hb), bite cells → acute hemolytic crisis. Reference: Harper's — G6PD RBC.
questions.conceptTag: G6PD NADPH RBC glutathione lethal deficiency

questions.questionText: Gluconeogenesis uses all of the following as precursors EXCEPT:
questions.optionB: Glycerol
questions.explanation: Gluconeogenesis precursors: lactate (Cori cycle), amino acids (alanine, glutamine — most important), glycerol (from lipolysis), propionate (odd-chain FA). Even-chain fatty acids CANNOT be used for net glucose synthesis (acetyl-CoA cannot be converted to pyruvate/OAA in mammals — no pyruvate synthase, no net conversion in TCA cycle). Odd-chain FA → propionyl-CoA → succinyl-CoA → OAA → glucose (minor contribution). Reference: Harper's — Gluconeogenesis.

questions.questionText: The irreversible enzymes of gluconeogenesis that bypass glycolytic steps include:
questions.optionB: Pyruvate carboxylase, PEPCK, fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase, glucose-6-phosphatase
questions.explanation: Four bypass enzymes replace the 3 irreversible glycolytic steps: (1) Pyruvate carboxylase (pyruvate → OAA, in mitochondria, requires biotin) + PEPCK (OAA → PEP) — bypasses pyruvate kinase. (2) Fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase (F-1,6-BP → F-6-P) — bypasses PFK-1. (3) Glucose-6-phosphatase (G-6-P → glucose, only in liver/kidney) — bypasses hexokinase. The remaining 7 glycolytic steps are reversible and shared. Reference: Harper's — Gluconeogenesis Enzymes.

questions.questionText: Glycogen synthase is activated by:
questions.optionB: Insulin (dephosphorylation by protein phosphatase-1)
questions.explanation: Glycogen synthase: active in DEPHOSPHORYLATED form (glycogen synthase a). Insulin activates protein phosphatase-1 → dephosphorylates glycogen synthase → activates it → glycogen synthesis. Glucagon/epinephrine → cAMP → PKA → phosphorylates glycogen synthase → INACTIVATES it (glycogen synthase b). Opposite regulation for glycogen phosphorylase: active when PHOSPHORYLATED (phosphorylase a). This reciprocal regulation prevents futile cycling. Reference: Harper's — Glycogen Regulation.

questions.questionText: Fluoroacetate poisoning is a classic example of lethal synthesis. The biochemical mechanism involves:
questions.optionB: Fluoroacetate is converted to fluorocitrate by citrate synthase → fluorocitrate irreversibly inhibits aconitase → TCA cycle blocked at citrate → complete halt of aerobic energy production
questions.optionC: Inhibition of Complex IV like cyanide
questions.explanation: Lethal synthesis — fluoroacetate: Step 1: Fluoroacetate + CoA → fluoroacetyl-CoA (by acetyl-CoA synthetase — cannot distinguish fluoroacetate from acetate). Step 2: Fluoroacetyl-CoA + OAA → FLUOROCITRATE (by citrate synthase — accepts the analog). This is 'lethal SYNTHESIS' — the toxin is metabolically activated. Step 3: Fluorocitrate irreversibly INHIBITS ACONITASE → citrate cannot be converted to isocitrate → TCA cycle BLOCKED. Result: citrate accumulates, ATP production stops (aerobic metabolism halted), death. Treatment: supportive (no specific antidote). Compare: (1) Arsenate: competes with phosphate (arsenolysis), (2) Arsenite: inhibits PDH/α-KGDH (lipoic acid binding), (3) Malonate: competitive inhibitor of SDH, (4) Cyanide: Complex IV. Reference: Harper's — Fluoroacetate.

questions.questionText: A patient with thiamine deficiency and lactic acidosis receives IV glucose, which worsens the acidosis. The biochemical explanation is:
questions.optionB: Glucose is metabolized via glycolysis to pyruvate, but pyruvate cannot enter TCA via PDH (requires TPP/thiamine) → pyruvate is shunted to lactate → worsening lactic acidosis; glucose administration without thiamine repletion is dangerous
questions.explanation: Glucose before thiamine — clinical disaster: Thiamine pyrophosphate (TPP) is a cofactor for: (1) PDH (pyruvate → acetyl-CoA), (2) α-KGDH (α-KG → succinyl-CoA), (3) Transketolase (HMP shunt), (4) BCKDH (branched-chain AA metabolism). In thiamine deficiency: PDH is impaired → pyruvate cannot enter aerobic metabolism. Give IV glucose → glycolysis produces MORE pyruvate → ALL shunted to LACTATE (LDH) → WORSENING lactic acidosis. Can precipitate acute Wernicke encephalopathy (ataxia, ophthalmoplegia, confusion). RULE: ALWAYS give THIAMINE BEFORE GLUCOSE in malnourished/alcoholic patients. This is one of the most tested clinical biochemistry concepts. Reference: Harper's — Thiamine Before Glucose.

questions.questionText: McArdle disease (GSD Type V) is caused by deficiency of:
questions.optionB: Muscle glycogen phosphorylase (myophosphorylase)
questions.explanation: McArdle disease (GSD V): muscle glycogen phosphorylase deficiency → cannot break down glycogen in MUSCLE → exercise intolerance, muscle cramps, myoglobinuria after intense exercise, no rise in blood lactate during ischemic forearm exercise test. Second wind phenomenon (switching to blood glucose/fatty acids). Liver glycogen metabolism is normal → no hypoglycemia. GSD VI (Hers): liver phosphorylase deficiency → hepatomegaly, mild hypoglycemia. Reference: Harper's — McArdle.

questions.questionText: Fructose-2,6-bisphosphate is the most potent activator of PFK-1 and is produced by:
questions.optionB: PFK-2 (phosphofructokinase-2/fructose-2,6-bisphosphatase, a bifunctional enzyme)
questions.explanation: Fructose-2,6-bisphosphate (F-2,6-BP): most potent allosteric activator of PFK-1 (glycolysis) and inhibitor of fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase (gluconeogenesis). Produced by the bifunctional enzyme PFK-2/FBPase-2. In fed state: insulin → activates PFK-2 (kinase activity) → more F-2,6-BP → glycolysis accelerated. In fasting: glucagon → cAMP → PKA → phosphorylates PFK-2 (activates bisphosphatase) → less F-2,6-BP → gluconeogenesis favored. Reference: Harper's — F-2,6-BP.

questions.questionText: The Warburg effect in cancer cells refers to:
questions.optionB: Increased glycolysis even in the presence of oxygen (aerobic glycolysis)
questions.explanation: Warburg effect (aerobic glycolysis): cancer cells preferentially use glycolysis even in the presence of adequate O2 → high glucose uptake, high lactate production. Advantages for cancer: rapid ATP generation (though less efficient), biosynthetic intermediates for growth, acidification of tumor microenvironment. Exploited clinically: PET scan uses 18F-FDG (fluorodeoxyglucose) — tumors with high glucose uptake light up. Reference: Harper's — Warburg Effect.

questions.questionText: G6PD deficiency causes hemolytic anemia because:
questions.optionB: Decreased NADPH → decreased reduced glutathione → RBCs susceptible to oxidative damage
questions.explanation: G6PD deficiency: most common enzyme deficiency worldwide (X-linked). G6PD is rate-limiting enzyme of HMP shunt → source of NADPH in RBCs. Low NADPH → cannot regenerate reduced glutathione (GSH) → RBCs vulnerable to oxidative stress → Heinz bodies (denatured Hb), bite cells → hemolysis. Triggered by: infections, drugs (primaquine, sulfonamides, nitrofurantoin), fava beans (favism), mothballs (naphthalene). Confers malaria protection. Reference: Harper's — G6PD Deficiency.

questions.questionText: Essential fructosuria is a benign condition due to deficiency of:
questions.optionB: Fructokinase (hepatic)
questions.explanation: Essential fructosuria: benign deficiency of fructokinase → fructose cannot be phosphorylated in liver → fructose excreted in urine. Asymptomatic. Hereditary fructose intolerance (HFI): aldolase B deficiency → fructose-1-phosphate accumulates → traps phosphate → inhibits glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis → hypoglycemia, liver damage, vomiting after fructose ingestion. Treatment: avoid fructose, sucrose, sorbitol. Reference: Harper's — Fructose Metabolism.

questions.questionText: Oxidative phosphorylation occurs at the:
questions.optionB: Inner mitochondrial membrane (electron transport chain and ATP synthase)
questions.explanation: Oxidative phosphorylation: inner mitochondrial membrane. ETC complexes: Complex I (NADH dehydrogenase), Complex II (succinate dehydrogenase), Complex III (cytochrome bc1), Complex IV (cytochrome c oxidase) → pump H+ out → proton gradient. Complex V (ATP synthase/F0F1) uses proton gradient to form ATP (chemiosmotic hypothesis — Peter Mitchell). NADH → 2.5 ATP, FADH2 → 1.5 ATP. Cyanide/CO inhibit Complex IV. Oligomycin inhibits ATP synthase. Uncouplers (DNP, thermogenin/UCP1) dissipate gradient. Reference: Harper's — Oxidative Phosphorylation.

questions.questionText: Glucokinase differs from hexokinase in that glucokinase:
questions.optionB: Has high Km (low affinity), is found in liver and pancreatic β cells, and is NOT inhibited by glucose-6-phosphate
questions.explanation: Glucokinase (hexokinase IV): liver and pancreatic β cells. High Km (~10 mM) → acts as glucose sensor. NOT inhibited by G6P product (unlike hexokinase). Induced by insulin. No allosteric inhibition by G6P → liver can continue to take up glucose after meals. Acts as glucose sensor in β cells → regulates insulin secretion (glucokinase = glucose sensor of β cell). MODY-2: glucokinase mutations → maturity-onset diabetes of the young. Reference: Harper's — Glucokinase.

questions.questionText: Pompe disease (GSD Type II) is caused by deficiency of:
questions.optionB: Lysosomal acid α-1,4-glucosidase (acid maltase)
questions.explanation: Pompe disease (GSD II): lysosomal acid maltase (acid α-glucosidase/GAA) deficiency → glycogen accumulates in lysosomes of ALL tissues, especially heart → massive cardiomegaly. Only GSD that is a lysosomal storage disease. Infantile form: severe cardiomyopathy, hypotonia ('floppy baby'), death by age 2. Late-onset: progressive myopathy. Treatment: enzyme replacement therapy (alglucosidase alfa). Reference: Harper's — Pompe Disease.

questions.questionText: The uronic acid pathway is important for synthesis of:
questions.optionB: Glucuronic acid (used for conjugation reactions in detoxification)
questions.explanation: Uronic acid pathway: glucose → G-6-P → G-1-P → UDP-glucose → UDP-glucuronic acid → glucuronide conjugates. Glucuronic acid is used for Phase II conjugation reactions: bilirubin glucuronidation (UGT), drug metabolism, steroid hormones clearance. Also precursor of L-ascorbic acid (vitamin C) in animals that can synthesize it — humans CANNOT (lack L-gulonolactone oxidase). Also involved in GAG synthesis. Reference: Harper's — Uronic Acid Pathway.

questions.questionText: D-lactic acidosis is a unique form of lactic acidosis seen in short bowel syndrome. The mechanism is:
questions.optionB: Unabsorbed carbohydrates reach the colon → fermented by bacteria into D-lactate → D-lactate is absorbed but humans lack efficient D-lactate dehydrogenase → accumulates causing neurological symptoms
questions.explanation: D-lactic acidosis: Humans produce L-lactate (by L-LDH). Unusual scenario in short bowel syndrome/jejunoileal bypass: (1) Malabsorbed carbohydrates reach COLON, (2) Colonic bacteria ferment carbs → produce D-LACTATE (bacterial LDH produces D-isomer), (3) D-lactate absorbed into blood, (4) Humans have very limited D-2-hydroxyacid dehydrogenase → D-lactate clears slowly → ACCUMULATES. Symptoms: episodic encephalopathy after high-carb meals (confusion, slurred speech, ataxia — mimics alcohol intoxication). Standard lactate assay measures only L-lactate → may MISS the diagnosis. Must order specific D-lactate level. Treatment: low-carb diet, oral antibiotics to reduce bacteria. Reference: Harper's — D-Lactic Acidosis.

questions.questionText: In fasting state, hepatic glucose output is maintained first predominantly by:
questions.optionB: Glycogenolysis followed by gluconeogenesis
questions.explanation: Correct answer: B. Early fasting relies on hepatic glycogenolysis; as fasting prolongs, gluconeogenesis becomes dominant. Glycolysis/lipogenesis are fed-state pathways.

questions.questionText: Fructose-2,6-bisphosphate in liver most strongly:
questions.optionB: Activates both PFK-1 and FBPase-1
questions.explanation: Correct answer: C. F2,6BP is a potent glycolysis promoter and gluconeogenesis suppressor: it activates PFK-1 and inhibits FBPase-1.

questions.questionText: Pyruvate carboxylase deficiency would most directly impair formation of:
questions.optionB: Oxaloacetate from pyruvate
questions.explanation: Correct answer: B. Pyruvate carboxylase converts pyruvate to oxaloacetate (biotin-dependent), crucial for gluconeogenesis and anaplerosis.

questions.questionText: The glucose-alanine cycle operates between:
questions.optionB: Muscle and liver (alanine carries amino groups from muscle to liver for urea synthesis)
questions.explanation: Glucose-alanine cycle: in muscle, pyruvate accepts amino groups (from muscle protein catabolism) via transamination → alanine. Alanine transported to liver → transaminated back to pyruvate (amino group to urea cycle) → pyruvate used for gluconeogenesis → glucose returns to muscle. Unlike the Cori cycle (which uses lactate), the alanine cycle transports both carbon skeletons AND nitrogen to the liver. Active during fasting and prolonged exercise. Reference: Harper's — Alanine Cycle.

questions.questionText: Substrate-level phosphorylation in glycolysis occurs at which two steps?
questions.optionB: Phosphoglycerate kinase (1,3-BPG → 3-PG) and pyruvate kinase (PEP → pyruvate)
questions.explanation: Substrate-level phosphorylation: ATP generated directly by transfer of a phosphoryl group from a substrate to ADP (no ETC required). In glycolysis: (1) Phosphoglycerate kinase: 1,3-BPG → 3-PG + ATP, (2) Pyruvate kinase: PEP → pyruvate + ATP. Each occurs twice per glucose → 4 ATP gross (minus 2 invested → net 2 ATP). In TCA: succinyl-CoA synthetase (GTP formation) is also substrate-level phosphorylation. Reference: Harper's — Substrate-Level Phosphorylation.

questions.questionText: A patient with cyanide poisoning is treated with hydroxocobalamin (vitamin B12a). The biochemical rationale is:
questions.optionB: Hydroxocobalamin's cobalt center has high affinity for CN⁻ → chelates cyanide forming cyanocobalamin (non-toxic) which is then renally excreted, effectively removing cyanide from Complex IV
questions.explanation: Cyanide antidotes — biochemical mechanisms: Cyanide binds Fe³+ in cytochrome c oxidase (Complex IV) → blocks ETC → cells cannot use O2 → cytotoxic hypoxia (high venous O2 sat, cherry-red color). Treatment options: (1) HYDROXOCOBALAMIN (Cyanokit): Co³+ in B12a has extremely high CN⁻ affinity → forms cyanocobalamin (vitamin B12!) → renally excreted. Preferred in smoke inhalation. (2) Nitrite-thiosulfate kit: sodium nitrite → oxidizes Hb Fe²+ to METHb Fe³+ → methemoglobin competes with Complex IV for CN⁻ → cyanmethemoglobin. Then sodium thiosulfate → rhodanese catalyzes CN⁻ + thiosulfate → thiocyanate (renally excreted). (3) Amyl nitrite: inhaled, also forms methemoglobin (first aid). Reference: Harper's — Cyanide Antidotes.

questions.questionText: Uncoupling proteins (UCP1/thermogenin) in brown adipose tissue produce heat by:
questions.optionB: Dissipating the proton gradient across the inner mitochondrial membrane without ATP synthesis
questions.explanation: UCP1 (thermogenin): creates a proton leak channel in the inner mitochondrial membrane → protons flow back without passing through ATP synthase → energy dissipated as HEAT instead of ATP. Essential for non-shivering thermogenesis in neonates and hibernating animals. Brown fat: rich in mitochondria (brown color from cytochromes). 2,4-DNP (dinitrophenol): synthetic uncoupler — once used as weight loss drug (dangerous — hyperthermia, death). Aspirin at high doses can also uncouple. Reference: Harper's — Uncoupling.

questions.questionText: Fluoride inhibits glycolysis by inhibiting which enzyme?
questions.optionB: Enolase (2-PG → PEP)
questions.explanation: Fluoride inhibits enolase (2-phosphoglycerate → phosphoenolpyruvate) by forming a complex with Mg2+ (cofactor). This is why sodium fluoride is used as an anticoagulant/preservative in blood glucose collection tubes — it prevents glycolysis by RBCs, keeping glucose levels stable for accurate measurement. Fluoride also inhibits ATPases. Arsenate uncouples substrate-level phosphorylation (replaces phosphate in glyceraldehyde-3-P dehydrogenase reaction → unstable 1-arseno-3-PG → no ATP at PGK step). Reference: Harper's — Glycolysis Inhibitors.

questions.questionText: GLUT4 transporter is unique because it is:
questions.optionB: Insulin-responsive — translocated to cell surface in response to insulin in muscle and adipose tissue
questions.optionD: Found only in liver and brain
questions.explanation: GLUT4: insulin-regulated glucose transporter in skeletal muscle and adipose tissue. Stored in intracellular vesicles → insulin binding activates PI3K/Akt pathway → GLUT4 vesicles translocate to plasma membrane → increases glucose uptake. In Type 2 DM: insulin resistance → impaired GLUT4 translocation. Exercise also promotes GLUT4 translocation (AMPK-mediated, insulin-independent). GLUT1: ubiquitous (RBCs, brain — basal uptake). GLUT2: liver, pancreatic β-cells (high Km). GLUT3: neurons (low Km, high affinity). Reference: Harper's — GLUT Transporters.

questions.questionText: Andersen disease (GSD Type IV) is caused by deficiency of:
questions.optionB: Branching enzyme (amylo-1,4→1,6-transglucosidase)
questions.explanation: Andersen disease (GSD IV): branching enzyme deficiency → abnormal glycogen with very long outer chains and few branch points (resembles amylopectin). This abnormal glycogen is insoluble → triggers immune response → progressive hepatic fibrosis → cirrhosis → liver failure. Usually fatal by age 5 without liver transplant. Not associated with significant hypoglycemia (unlike GSD I). Least common of the hepatic GSDs. Reference: Harper's — Andersen Disease.

questions.questionText: In the fed state, insulin activates protein phosphatase 1 (PP1) in the liver. The integrated metabolic effect is:
questions.optionB: Dephosphorylation simultaneously ACTIVATES glycogen synthase (promoting glycogen synthesis) and INACTIVATES glycogen phosphorylase + phosphorylase kinase (stopping glycogenolysis) — a coordinated switch
questions.explanation: Insulin → PP1 — coordinated metabolic switch: Insulin (fed state) activates PP1 (protein phosphatase 1) which simultaneously: (1) DEPHOSPHORYLATES glycogen synthase → ACTIVE form (synthase a) → glycogen SYNTHESIS ON, (2) DEPHOSPHORYLATES phosphorylase kinase → INACTIVE → cannot activate glycogen phosphorylase, (3) DEPHOSPHORYLATES glycogen phosphorylase a → phosphorylase b (INACTIVE) → glycogenolysis OFF. This is an elegant example of RECIPROCAL REGULATION via a single enzyme (PP1). Glucagon does the opposite: activates PKA → phosphorylates all three targets in the opposing direction. PP1 is targeted to glycogen by specific regulatory subunits (GL in liver, GM/RGL in muscle). Reference: Harper's — PP1 Glycogen Regulation.

questions.questionText: Hereditary fructose intolerance (HFI) differs from essential fructosuria in that HFI:
questions.optionB: Is caused by aldolase B deficiency leading to severe hypoglycemia and liver damage upon fructose ingestion
questions.optionD: Does not produce any urine abnormality
questions.explanation: HFI: aldolase B deficiency → fructose-1-phosphate (F1P) accumulates in liver → (1) traps phosphate (Pi sequestration) → inhibits glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis → SEVERE hypoglycemia, (2) inhibits aldolase A → blocks glycolysis, (3) hepatotoxicity (vomiting, jaundice, liver failure). Essential fructosuria: fructokinase deficiency → fructose in urine, completely benign. HFI treatment: avoid fructose, sucrose, and sorbitol. F1P also depletes ATP → hyperuricemia. Reference: Harper's — HFI vs Essential Fructosuria.

questions.questionText: Isocitrate dehydrogenase (IDH) mutations are found in gliomas and AML. Mutant IDH produces a novel oncometabolite:
questions.optionB: 2-hydroxyglutarate (2-HG) — a competitive inhibitor of α-KG-dependent dioxygenases including TET2 (DNA demethylation) and JmjC histone demethylases, causing hypermethylation and blocked differentiation
questions.explanation: IDH mutations — oncometabolism: Normal: isocitrate → α-ketoglutarate (α-KG) + CO2 + NADPH (by IDH1 cytoplasmic, IDH2 mitochondrial). Mutant IDH (neomorphic gain-of-function): α-KG → 2-HYDROXYGLUTARATE (2-HG) using NADPH. 2-HG = oncometabolite: structural analog of α-KG → COMPETITIVELY INHIBITS α-KG-dependent dioxygenases: (1) TET2: DNA hydroxymethylation (demethylation pathway) → hypermethylation → gene silencing, (2) JmjC histone demethylases → histone hypermethylation → blocked differentiation, (3) PHDs (prolyl hydroxylases) → HIF stabilization → pseudo-hypoxic gene expression. Result: epigenetic reprogramming → differentiation block → cancer. Glioma CpG island methylator phenotype (G-CIMP): driven by IDH mutations. Vorasidenib/ivosidenib: IDH inhibitors (targeted therapy for IDH-mutant gliomas, AML). Reference: Harper's — IDH Oncometabolite.

questions.questionText: Arsenate poisoning affects glycolysis by:
questions.optionB: Replacing inorganic phosphate in the G3PDH reaction → forming unstable 1-arseno-3-phosphoglycerate → bypassing ATP generation at the PGK step
questions.optionD: Blocking the pyruvate kinase step only
questions.explanation: Arsenate (AsO4^3-) mimics phosphate (PO4^3-) structurally. In glycolysis: replaces Pi in the glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase (G3PDH) reaction → 1-arseno-3-phosphoglycerate is formed instead of 1,3-BPG → spontaneously hydrolyzes (unstable) → bypasses the phosphoglycerate kinase step → NO ATP generated at that step (arsenolysis). Net ATP yield from glycolysis drops from 2 to 0. Arsenic also inhibits PDH and α-KGDH by binding lipoic acid. Reference: Harper's — Arsenate Poisoning.

questions.questionText: Which GSD is the only lysosomal storage disease?
questions.optionB: GSD II (Pompe disease — acid maltase/acid α-glucosidase deficiency)
questions.explanation: Pompe disease (GSD II) is unique among GSDs: it is the ONLY one that is a lysosomal storage disease. Acid maltase (acid α-glucosidase/GAA) is a lysosomal enzyme → its deficiency causes glycogen accumulation within lysosomes of all tissues. All other GSDs involve cytoplasmic glycogen metabolism enzymes. Pompe is also the only GSD with massive cardiomegaly. Enzyme replacement therapy (alglucosidase alfa) is available. Newborn screening for Pompe is now available. Reference: Harper's — GSD Classification.

questions.questionText: Anaplerotic reactions replenish TCA cycle intermediates. The most important anaplerotic reaction is catalyzed by:
questions.optionB: Pyruvate carboxylase (pyruvate → oxaloacetate, requires biotin)
questions.explanation: Pyruvate carboxylase: most important anaplerotic enzyme. Converts pyruvate → oxaloacetate (OAA) in mitochondria. Requires biotin as cofactor and ATP. Allosteric activator: acetyl-CoA (signals that more OAA is needed for citrate synthase to condense with acetyl-CoA). Without anaplerosis, TCA cycle intermediates would be depleted as they are removed for biosynthesis (gluconeogenesis, amino acid synthesis, heme synthesis). Other anaplerotic reactions: glutamate → α-KG, aspartate → OAA. Reference: Harper's — Anaplerosis.

questions.questionText: During prolonged starvation (>3 days), the brain adapts to use which alternative fuel?
questions.optionB: Ketone bodies (acetoacetate and β-hydroxybutyrate)
questions.explanation: During prolonged starvation: liver glycogen depleted by 24 hours. Gluconeogenesis from amino acids maintains blood glucose initially but would deplete muscle protein rapidly. By day 3-4: liver ketogenesis increases from fatty acids → ketone bodies (acetoacetate, β-hydroxybutyrate) cross blood-brain barrier → brain adapts to use ketones for up to 60-70% of energy needs → protein sparing effect. Fatty acids CANNOT cross the blood-brain barrier (albumin-bound). This adaptation is critical for survival. Reference: Harper's — Starvation Biochemistry.

questions.questionText: The malate-aspartate shuttle transfers reducing equivalents from cytoplasmic NADH to mitochondria and yields:
questions.optionB: 2.5 ATP per NADH (as mitochondrial NADH)
questions.explanation: Malate-aspartate shuttle: operates in heart and liver. Cytoplasmic NADH → reduces OAA to malate (malate DH) → malate enters mitochondria → reoxidized to OAA (generating mitochondrial NADH → 2.5 ATP). More efficient than glycerol-3-phosphate shuttle (muscle/brain: cytoplasmic NADH → generates FADH2 → only 1.5 ATP). This is why aerobic oxidation of glucose yields 30-32 ATP (malate-aspartate shuttle) or 28-30 ATP (glycerol-3-P shuttle). Reference: Harper's — NADH Shuttles.

questions.questionText: Galactokinase deficiency differs from classical galactosemia in that galactokinase deficiency causes:
questions.optionA: Severe liver failure and intellectual disability
questions.optionB: Cataracts only (no liver or brain damage)
questions.explanation: Galactokinase deficiency (non-classic galactosemia): mild. Galactose cannot be phosphorylated → accumulates → converted to galactitol by aldose reductase in lens → osmotic swelling → cataracts (bilateral, juvenile). NO liver damage, NO intellectual disability (unlike classical galactosemia — Gal-1-P uridylyltransferase deficiency, which causes all of these). Galactitol is the sugar alcohol causing cataracts in both galactokinase deficiency and classical galactosemia (analogous to sorbitol in diabetes). Reference: Harper's — Galactokinase Deficiency.

questions.questionText: The Pasteur effect refers to:
questions.optionA: Increased glucose consumption in cancer cells
questions.optionB: Inhibition of glycolysis by oxygen (decreased glucose consumption when switching from anaerobic to aerobic conditions)
questions.optionD: Conversion of lactate to glucose in liver
questions.explanation: Pasteur effect: decrease in the rate of glucose consumption when cells/organisms switch from anaerobic to aerobic conditions. Mechanism: aerobic conditions → ATP production via oxidative phosphorylation is much more efficient → ATP and citrate levels rise → inhibit PFK-1 → glycolysis slows down → less glucose consumed. Opposite of Warburg effect (cancer cells maintain high glycolysis even with O2). The Pasteur effect is seen in yeast (fermentation stops when O2 available). Reference: Harper's — Pasteur Effect.

questions.questionText: Hers disease (GSD Type VI) is caused by deficiency of:
questions.optionB: Liver glycogen phosphorylase
questions.explanation: Hers disease (GSD VI): liver phosphorylase deficiency → hepatomegaly (glycogen accumulation in liver), mild fasting hypoglycemia, growth retardation. Relatively benign prognosis compared to other hepatic GSDs. Contrast with McArdle (GSD V): MUSCLE phosphorylase deficiency → exercise intolerance, no hypoglycemia. GSD IX (most common GSD): phosphorylase kinase deficiency → similar to GSD VI but even milder. Memory: VI = liver (hepatic), V = muscle (myopathic). Reference: Harper's — Hers Disease.

questions.questionText: A child with exercise intolerance, muscle cramps, and myoglobinuria but NO rise in blood lactate during the ischemic forearm exercise test most likely has:
questions.optionB: McArdle disease (GSD V)
questions.explanation: McArdle disease clinical vignette: myophosphorylase deficiency → cannot break down muscle glycogen → exercise intolerance, painful cramps during intense exercise, myoglobinuria (dark urine from muscle damage), second wind phenomenon. Ischemic forearm exercise test: NO rise in blood lactate (cannot produce lactate from glycogen), but ammonia levels rise normally. Blood glucose is NORMAL (liver glycogenolysis intact). Creatine kinase elevated during attacks. Reference: Harper's — McArdle Diagnosis.

questions.questionText: Transaldolase deficiency (a rare HMP shunt non-oxidative phase defect) presents differently from G6PD deficiency because:
questions.optionA: It does not involve the HMP shunt
questions.optionB: Transaldolase deficiency causes liver disease (hepatosplenomegaly, fibrosis) due to accumulation of sedoheptulose-7-phosphate and erythritol — NOT hemolysis, since NADPH production (oxidative phase) is intact
questions.explanation: HMP shunt: oxidative vs non-oxidative phase defects: OXIDATIVE phase (G6PD, 6PGD): produces NADPH. G6PD deficiency → decreased NADPH → decreased glutathione → hemolytic crises. NON-OXIDATIVE phase (transketolase, transaldolase): rearranges sugar phosphates. Transaldolase deficiency: NADPH production INTACT → no hemolysis. Instead: accumulation of sedoheptulose-7-phosphate and downstream polyols (sedoheptitol, erythritol) → HEPATOPATHY (hepatosplenomegaly, liver fibrosis, thrombocytopenia). Neonatal presentation: hydrops fetalis in severe cases. Transketolase: requires TPP (thiamine). Key concept: oxidative = NADPH = protective against ROS; non-oxidative = sugar rearrangement = synthetic pathway. Reference: Harper's — Transaldolase Deficiency.

questions.questionText: Complex I inhibition by rotenone causes ATP depletion. However, cells can partially bypass this block using which metabolic strategy?
questions.optionB: Providing succinate (entering ETC at Complex II via FADH2) or using glycerol-3-phosphate shuttle (donates electrons to CoQ via mitochondrial G3P dehydrogenase), both bypassing Complex I
questions.explanation: ETC bypass strategies — Complex I: Rotenone, metformin (partial), barbiturates inhibit Complex I (NADH → ubiquinone). Bypass mechanisms: (1) SUCCINATE: oxidized by Complex II (succinate dehydrogenase) → FADH2 → electrons enter CoQ DIRECTLY, bypassing Complex I entirely. (2) Glycerol-3-phosphate shuttle: cytoplasmic NADH → G3P (cytoplasmic G3PDH) → mitochondrial G3PDH (FAD-linked, outer surface IMM) → donates electrons directly to CoQ. (3) ETF (electron transfer flavoprotein): from fatty acid β-oxidation FADH2 → ETF → ETF-QO → CoQ. All bypass Complex I → maintain some electron flow → some ATP (but less, since Complex I proton pumping is lost). Similarly, antimycin A (Complex III blocker) can be bypassed by alternative oxidase in plants but NOT in mammals. Reference: Harper's — ETC Complexes.
questions.conceptTag: Complex I bypass succinate G3P shuttle

questions.questionText: 2,3-Bisphosphoglycerate (2,3-BPG) is produced in RBCs by the Rapoport-Luebering shunt and functions to:
questions.optionB: Decrease oxygen affinity of hemoglobin (shifts O2 dissociation curve to the right → facilitates O2 release to tissues)
questions.explanation: 2,3-BPG (2,3-DPG): produced from 1,3-BPG by bisphosphoglycerate mutase in RBCs (Rapoport-Luebering shunt). Binds to deoxyhemoglobin (T-state) in the central cavity between β chains → stabilizes T-state → decreases O2 affinity → RIGHT shift of O2 dissociation curve → promotes O2 unloading at tissues. Increased 2,3-BPG: chronic hypoxia (high altitude, chronic lung disease, anemia). Decreased in stored blood (important for transfusion medicine). Fetal hemoglobin (HbF) binds 2,3-BPG poorly → higher O2 affinity. Reference: Harper's — 2,3-BPG.

questions.questionText: Von Gierke disease (GSD I) presents with all the following EXCEPT:
questions.optionB: Hepatomegaly
questions.explanation: Von Gierke (GSD I): glucose-6-phosphatase deficiency. Features: severe fasting hypoglycemia (cannot release free glucose from G6P), hepatomegaly and renomegaly (glycogen + fat accumulation), lactic acidosis (excess G6P → glycolysis → lactate), hyperuricemia (increased purine degradation from high G6P flux through HMP shunt), hyperlipidemia (excess acetyl-CoA → lipogenesis). Massive CARDIOMEGALY is characteristic of Pompe disease (GSD II), NOT Von Gierke. Doll-like facies in GSD I. Reference: Harper's — Von Gierke Features.

questions.questionText: Phosphoenolpyruvate carboxykinase (PEPCK) is regulated primarily at the level of:
questions.optionB: Gene transcription (induced by glucagon/cortisol, repressed by insulin)
questions.explanation: PEPCK: key gluconeogenic enzyme (OAA → PEP + CO2 + GTP). Primarily controlled at transcriptional level. Glucagon (via CREB) and cortisol (via GR) induce PEPCK gene expression → increased gluconeogenesis during fasting and stress. Insulin represses PEPCK transcription → decreased gluconeogenesis in fed state. Unlike PFK-1 (mainly allosteric regulation), PEPCK is regulated largely by enzyme quantity (slow response — hours rather than seconds). Reference: Harper's — PEPCK Regulation.

questions.questionText: The Crabtree effect in tumor cells refers to:
questions.optionB: Inhibition of oxidative phosphorylation by high glucose availability even in the presence of oxygen
questions.explanation: Crabtree effect: suppression of mitochondrial respiration (oxidative phosphorylation) by high glucose concentrations, complementing the Warburg effect. When glucose is abundant, glycolytic intermediates and ATP from increased glycolysis inhibit mitochondrial oxidative pathways. Both effects result in cancer cells preferring glycolysis. This is distinct from the Pasteur effect (where aerobic conditions suppress glycolysis). The Crabtree effect is also seen in yeast fermentation. Reference: Harper's — Metabolic Effects in Cancer.

questions.questionText: Glycogen phosphorylase is activated by phosphorylation via which kinase cascade?
questions.optionB: Phosphorylase kinase (activated by PKA in response to glucagon/epinephrine via cAMP)
questions.explanation: Glycogenolysis cascade: Glucagon/Epinephrine → G-protein → adenylate cyclase → cAMP → PKA → phosphorylase kinase (activates by phosphorylation) → glycogen phosphorylase b → phosphorylase a (active, phosphorylated). Calcium also activates phosphorylase kinase directly (δ subunit = calmodulin — important in muscle contraction). This amplification cascade ensures rapid glycogen mobilization. Each step amplifies the signal ~10-fold. Glycogen phosphorylase cleaves α1→4 bonds (releasing glucose-1-phosphate). Reference: Harper's — Glycogenolysis Cascade.

questions.questionText: Which enzyme is common to both glycolysis and gluconeogenesis?
questions.optionB: Phosphoglycerate kinase (reversible enzyme)
questions.explanation: Of the 10 glycolytic reactions, 7 are reversible and SHARED with gluconeogenesis. Phosphoglycerate kinase is one of these shared enzymes (catalyzes 1,3-BPG ↔ 3-PG, reversible). The 3 irreversible glycolytic enzymes (hexokinase, PFK-1, pyruvate kinase) are replaced by gluconeogenic bypass enzymes (G6Pase, FBPase-1, pyruvate carboxylase + PEPCK). Other shared enzymes include aldolase, triose phosphate isomerase, G3PDH, phosphoglycerate mutase, enolase. Reference: Harper's — Shared Glycolysis/Gluconeogenesis Steps.

questions.questionText: Antimycin A inhibits electron transport chain at:
questions.optionB: Complex III (cytochrome bc1 complex)
questions.explanation: Antimycin A: binds to the Qi site of Complex III (cytochrome bc1/ubiquinol-cytochrome c oxidoreductase) → blocks electron transfer from cytochrome b to ubiquinone (Q cycle). This halts electron flow from both Complex I (NADH) and Complex II (FADH2) pathways since both converge at ubiquinone before Complex III. Complete ETC blockade. Summary: Complex I (rotenone, barbiturates), Complex II (malonate), Complex III (antimycin A), Complex IV (CN-, CO, H2S), Complex V/ATP synthase (oligomycin). Reference: Harper's — Complex III Inhibition.

questions.questionText: A rapidly growing tumor shows high glucose uptake on PET scan despite adequate oxygen supply. This phenomenon of aerobic glycolysis in cancer cells is known as:
questions.optionB: Warburg effect
questions.explanation: Warburg effect (aerobic glycolysis): cancer cells preferentially use glycolysis even in the presence of adequate O2 → produce lactate instead of fully oxidizing glucose via TCA/ETC. Advantages for cancer: (1) rapid ATP generation (glycolysis is faster though less efficient), (2) glycolytic intermediates diverted to biosynthetic pathways (nucleotides, lipids, amino acids for rapid proliferation), (3) acidic microenvironment (lactate) promotes invasion/metastasis. HIF-1α upregulates glycolytic enzymes and GLUT1. PET scan uses 18F-FDG (fluorodeoxyglucose) — taken up by GLUT1/3 → trapped after phosphorylation by hexokinase (not metabolized further). Pasteur effect: O2 inhibits glycolysis (opposite). Crabtree effect: high glucose inhibits respiration in some cells. Reference: Harper's — Cancer Metabolism.

questions.questionText: A malnourished patient admitted to the ICU develops cardiac arrhythmia and respiratory failure 48 hours after starting parenteral nutrition. Serum phosphate is critically low. The most likely diagnosis is:
questions.optionB: Refeeding syndrome
questions.explanation: Refeeding syndrome: life-threatening metabolic complication when nutrition is restarted in chronically malnourished/starved patients. Mechanism: insulin surge from carbohydrate load → drives K+, PO4³⁻, Mg²⁺ INTO cells → severe HYPOphosphatemia (most dangerous — ATP/2,3-BPG synthesis impaired → cardiac/respiratory failure, rhabdomyolysis), hypokalemia (arrhythmias), hypomagnesemia. Also increased thiamine demand (TPP needed for carbohydrate metabolism) → may precipitate Wernicke encephalopathy. Prevention: start feeding slowly ('start low, go slow'), supplement phosphate/K+/Mg²+ and thiamine BEFORE refeeding. At risk: anorexia nervosa, chronic alcoholism, prolonged fasting, cancer cachexia. Reference: Harper's — Refeeding Syndrome.

questions.questionText: Mature red blood cells generate ATP exclusively through glycolysis because they:
questions.optionB: Lack mitochondria and therefore cannot perform oxidative phosphorylation or TCA cycle
questions.explanation: Mature RBCs: anucleate, NO mitochondria → cannot perform TCA cycle, β-oxidation, oxidative phosphorylation. Energy production: EXCLUSIVELY anaerobic glycolysis (Embden-Meyerhof pathway → 2 ATP/glucose). Key RBC-specific features: (1) 2,3-BPG synthesis via Rapoport-Luebering shunt (bisphosphoglycerate mutase: 1,3-BPG → 2,3-BPG → 3-PG, bypasses PGK step → sacrifices 1 ATP for 2,3-BPG production). 2,3-BPG decreases Hb-O2 affinity → facilitates O2 release to tissues. (2) HMP shunt (G6PD) generates NADPH → maintains GSH → protects against oxidative damage (G6PD deficiency → hemolytic anemia). (3) Methemoglobin reductase (NADH-dependent) keeps Fe²+. Reference: Harper's — RBC Metabolism.

questions.questionText: A child presents with seizures, developmental delay, and low CSF glucose with normal blood glucose. The most likely diagnosis involves deficiency of:
questions.optionB: GLUT1 transporter (blood-brain barrier glucose transporter)
questions.explanation: GLUT1 deficiency syndrome (De Vivo disease): autosomal dominant SLC2A1 mutation → defective glucose transport across the blood-brain barrier → brain glucose deprivation despite normal blood glucose. CSF glucose/blood glucose ratio < 0.4 (normally 0.6). Features: infantile seizures (refractory to anticonvulsants), developmental delay, microcephaly, movement disorders. Treatment: KETOGENIC DIET (ketone bodies bypass GLUT1, cross BBB via MCT1 → provide brain fuel). GLUT transporter distribution: GLUT1 (BBB, RBCs — insulin-independent, low Km), GLUT2 (liver, pancreatic β-cells, kidney — high Km, bidirectional), GLUT3 (neurons — lowest Km), GLUT4 (muscle, adipose — insulin-dependent), GLUT5 (intestine — fructose). Reference: Harper's — GLUT1 Deficiency.

questions.questionText: Fructose-2,6-bisphosphate (F-2,6-BP) is the most potent allosteric activator of PFK-1. In the fasting state, hepatic F-2,6-BP levels decrease because:
questions.optionB: Glucagon → cAMP → PKA phosphorylates the bifunctional enzyme PFK-2/FBPase-2 → PFK-2 activity decreases and FBPase-2 activity increases → F-2,6-BP is hydrolyzed
questions.explanation: F-2,6-BP regulation — critical integration point: The bifunctional enzyme PFK-2/FBPase-2 controls F-2,6-BP levels. Fed state (insulin): PFK-2/FBPase-2 is DEPHOSPHORYLATED → PFK-2 is ACTIVE → makes F-2,6-BP → activates PFK-1 → promotes glycolysis. Fasted state (glucagon → cAMP → PKA): PFK-2/FBPase-2 is PHOSPHORYLATED → PFK-2 is INACTIVE, FBPase-2 is ACTIVE → F-2,6-BP is degraded → PFK-1 less active + FBPase-1 uninhibited → gluconeogenesis favored. TRAP: In LIVER, phosphorylation INACTIVATES PFK-2. In HEART, phosphorylation ACTIVATES PFK-2 (different isoform) → ensures heart maintains glycolysis during stress. This is a common NEET PG trap. Reference: Harper's — F-2,6-BP Regulation.

questions.questionText: A patient with metformin overdose presents with severe lactic acidosis. This is classified as type B lactic acidosis because:
questions.optionA: It is caused by tissue hypoxia from shock
questions.optionB: It occurs WITHOUT tissue hypoxia — metformin inhibits Complex I of ETC → impairs oxidative metabolism → forces anaerobic glycolysis
questions.optionD: It results from hepatic glycogen excess
questions.explanation: Lactic acidosis classification: Type A (most common): tissue HYPOXIA → inadequate O2 for ETC → anaerobic glycolysis → lactate. Causes: shock, severe anemia, cardiac failure, severe hypoxemia. Type B: NO tissue hypoxia — metabolic/toxic causes. Type B1: underlying disease (liver failure, malignancy, DKA, sepsis without shock). Type B2: drugs/toxins (metformin → Complex I inhibition, cyanide → Complex IV, antiretrovirals → mitochondrial toxicity, salicylate, propofol). Type B3: inborn errors (PDH deficiency, mitochondrial myopathies, G6Pase deficiency). Metformin lactic acidosis risk increases with renal impairment (drug accumulates) — contraindicated if eGFR < 30. Reference: Harper's — Lactic Acidosis Classification.
questions.conceptTag: Lactic acidosis type A vs B classification

questions.questionText: The Pasteur effect refers to:
questions.optionA: Increased glycolysis in the presence of oxygen
questions.optionB: Inhibition of glycolysis when oxygen becomes available — cells switch from fermentation to oxidative phosphorylation
questions.optionD: Inhibition of TCA cycle by excess NADH
questions.explanation: Pasteur effect: in the presence of O2, cells shift from anaerobic glycolysis (fermentation) to aerobic metabolism (oxidative phosphorylation) → glycolysis rate DECREASES because: (1) ATP from OxPhos allosterically inhibits PFK-1, (2) citrate (from active TCA) also inhibits PFK-1, (3) cells don't need to consume as much glucose (OxPhos yields 30-32 ATP vs 2 ATP from glycolysis). The Warburg effect in cancer is essentially a LOSS of the Pasteur effect — cancer cells continue high glycolysis despite O2 availability. Crabtree effect: in some cells (e.g., yeast, rapidly proliferating cells), high glucose concentration suppresses respiration even with O2 present. These three effects (Pasteur, Warburg, Crabtree) are commonly compared in NEET PG. Reference: Harper's — Pasteur Effect.

questions.questionText: Pyruvate dehydrogenase complex (PDH) is ACTIVE in which metabolic state?
questions.optionA: Fasting state when glucagon is dominant
questions.optionB: Fed state when insulin is dominant — PDH is active in its DEPHOSPHORYLATED form
questions.optionC: During prolonged exercise when acetyl-CoA levels are very high
questions.explanation: PDH regulation — common NEET PG trap: PDH is ACTIVE when DEPHOSPHORYLATED (opposite of glycogen phosphorylase!). Fed state (insulin): PDH phosphatase activated → removes phosphate → PDH ACTIVE → pyruvate → acetyl-CoA flows into TCA cycle. Fasting state: PDH kinase activated by acetyl-CoA, NADH, ATP → phosphorylates PDH → PDH INACTIVE → pyruvate spared for gluconeogenesis. Key trap: students often confuse — phosphorylation activates glycogen phosphorylase but INACTIVATES PDH and glycogen synthase. PDH is INACTIVATED by: (1) its products (acetyl-CoA, NADH), (2) ATP, (3) phosphorylation. PDH is ACTIVATED by: (1) its substrates (pyruvate, CoA, NAD+), (2) ADP, Ca²+, (3) dephosphorylation (insulin stimulates phosphatase). Reference: Harper's — PDH Regulation.

questions.questionText: A patient with Fanconi-Bickel syndrome presents with hepatomegaly, fasting hypoglycemia, glycogen accumulation, and proximal renal tubular dysfunction. The defective transporter is:
questions.optionB: GLUT2
questions.explanation: Fanconi-Bickel syndrome (GSD type XI): autosomal recessive GLUT2 (SLC2A2) mutation. GLUT2 is a high-Km, bidirectional glucose transporter in liver, kidney proximal tubule, pancreatic β-cells, and intestinal basolateral membrane. Deficiency → (1) Liver: glucose cannot exit hepatocytes → glycogen accumulates → hepatomegaly + fasting hypoglycemia (glucose trapped in liver, cannot be released to blood), (2) Kidney: impaired tubular glucose reabsorption → glycosuria + proximal tubular dysfunction (renal Fanconi syndrome), (3) Intestine: impaired glucose/galactose absorption. Distinguishing from other GSDs: glycogen accumulation WITH renal tubular defects is characteristic. Unlike Von Gierke (G6Pase deficiency), enzyme activities are normal. Reference: Harper's — GLUT2 Deficiency.

questions.questionText: The irreversible steps of glycolysis are catalyzed by all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.optionB: Phosphofructokinase-1 (PFK-1)
questions.explanation: Glycolysis has 3 irreversible (committed) steps — all are regulatory points: (1) Hexokinase/Glucokinase: Glucose → G6P (ATP used), (2) PFK-1: F6P → F-1,6-BP (rate-limiting, most regulated — activated by AMP, ADP, F-2,6-BP; inhibited by ATP, citrate), (3) Pyruvate kinase: PEP → pyruvate (generates ATP). These 3 steps are bypassed in gluconeogenesis by DIFFERENT enzymes: G6Pase, FBPase-1, and pyruvate carboxylase + PEPCK respectively. Phosphoglycerate kinase (3-PG ↔ 1,3-BPG) is a REVERSIBLE step — it functions in BOTH glycolysis and gluconeogenesis. Common trap: students may confuse reversible and irreversible steps. The 7 reversible steps are shared between glycolysis and gluconeogenesis. Reference: Harper's — Irreversible Steps of Glycolysis.

questions.questionText: During intense exercise, the skeletal muscle transitions through fuel sources in which order?
questions.optionB: Creatine phosphate (ATP-PCr system, 0-10 seconds) → anaerobic glycolysis (10 sec - 2 min) → aerobic oxidation of fatty acids and glucose (> 2 min)
questions.explanation: Exercise fuel switching — timed phases: Phase 1 (0-10 sec, immediate): ATP-PCr system. Pre-formed ATP (lasts ~2 sec) then creatine phosphate + ADP → ATP + creatine (creatine kinase). Fastest but shortest supply. Phase 2 (10 sec-2 min, short-term): Anaerobic glycolysis (muscle glycogen → glucose → pyruvate → lactate). Generates 2 ATP rapidly. Lactate accumulation → muscle fatigue. Phase 3 (>2 min, long-term): Aerobic metabolism — initially muscle glycogen + blood glucose, progressively more fatty acid β-oxidation (>30 min exercise). Fatty acids become dominant fuel at moderate intensity/prolonged exercise. AMPK activated during exercise → increases FA oxidation, GLUT4 translocation. VO2 max determines aerobic capacity. Reference: Harper's — Exercise Biochemistry.

questions.questionText: 2,3-Bisphosphoglycerate (2,3-BPG) is synthesized in RBCs by the Rapoport-Luebering shunt. Which clinical condition causes DECREASED 2,3-BPG leading to increased hemoglobin-oxygen affinity?
questions.optionB: Stored/banked blood (2,3-BPG degrades during storage → left-shifted O2 dissociation curve)
questions.explanation: 2,3-BPG and O2 affinity: 2,3-BPG binds to the central cavity of deoxyhemoglobin (between β-chains) → stabilizes T-state → DECREASES O2 affinity → RIGHT shift → promotes O2 release to tissues. Increased 2,3-BPG (right shift, less affinity): chronic hypoxia, high altitude, anemia, chronic lung disease, thyroid hormones. Decreased 2,3-BPG (left shift, more affinity): (1) STORED/BANKED blood — 2,3-BPG degrades within 1-2 weeks of storage → transfused blood has high O2 affinity → impaired O2 delivery (clinically important in massive transfusions), (2) Hexokinase deficiency in RBCs, (3) Septic shock. Fetal hemoglobin (HbF): does NOT bind 2,3-BPG well (γ-chains lack binding site) → higher O2 affinity → facilitates placental O2 transfer. Reference: Harper's — 2,3-BPG Regulation.

questions.questionText: A patient with pyruvate carboxylase deficiency would show impaired function of which pathways?
questions.optionB: Gluconeogenesis (pyruvate → OAA), TCA cycle anaplerosis, and lipogenesis (OAA required for citrate synthesis)
questions.optionD: Purine synthesis and urea cycle only
questions.explanation: Pyruvate carboxylase: biotin-dependent, mitochondrial enzyme. Pyruvate + CO2 + ATP → oxaloacetate (OAA). Critical at the intersection of MULTIPLE pathways: (1) Gluconeogenesis: first step → OAA → PEP (PEPCK) → glucose. Deficiency → fasting hypoglycemia + lactic acidosis (pyruvate accumulates → lactate). (2) TCA cycle anaplerosis: replenishes OAA consumed by biosynthetic reactions. Deficiency → impaired TCA function. (3) Lipogenesis: OAA + acetyl-CoA → citrate (citrate synthase) → exported for FA synthesis. (4) Aspartate synthesis: OAA + glutamate → aspartate (needed for urea cycle and nucleotide synthesis). Clinical features: severe lactic acidosis, hyperammonemia (impaired urea cycle from low aspartate), developmental delay. Cofactor: biotin. Reference: Harper's — Pyruvate Carboxylase.

questions.questionText: SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin) reduce blood glucose in diabetic patients by:
questions.optionA: Stimulating insulin secretion from pancreatic β-cells
questions.optionB: Blocking sodium-glucose co-transporter 2 in the proximal convoluted tubule → reducing renal glucose reabsorption → inducing glycosuria
questions.explanation: SGLT2 inhibitors — mechanism: Normally, ~180 g glucose is filtered daily by kidneys → ~90% reabsorbed by SGLT2 (low affinity, high capacity, PCT S1/S2 segments) → ~10% by SGLT1 (high affinity, low capacity, PCT S3 and intestine). SGLT2 inhibitors block this reabsorption → induce glycosuria (~70-80 g/day) → lower blood glucose independently of insulin secretion. Additional benefits: weight loss (caloric loss in urine), blood pressure reduction (osmotic diuresis, natriuresis), cardiovascular and renal protective effects (reduced heart failure, slowed CKD). Side effects: UTI/genital infections (glycosuria promotes microbial growth), euglycemic DKA (rare but dangerous — occurs without significant hyperglycemia), volume depletion. Reference: Harper's — Renal Glucose Handling.

questions.questionText: Dinitrophenol (DNP) and thermogenin (UCP1) both cause uncoupling of oxidative phosphorylation. The key difference is:
questions.optionA: DNP inhibits Complex IV while UCP1 inhibits Complex I
questions.optionB: Both dissipate the proton gradient, but UCP1 is a regulated physiological uncoupler in brown adipose tissue for thermogenesis, while DNP is an uncontrolled chemical uncoupler
questions.optionC: UCP1 increases ATP synthesis while DNP decreases it
questions.optionD: DNP acts in the matrix while UCP1 acts in the cytoplasm
questions.explanation: Uncouplers dissipate the proton gradient across the inner mitochondrial membrane → protons flow back WITHOUT passing through ATP synthase → energy released as HEAT instead of ATP. UCP1 (thermogenin): physiological uncoupler in brown adipose tissue (BAT) → non-shivering thermogenesis (important in neonates and hibernating animals). Regulated by: norepinephrine (via β3-adrenergic receptors → increases lipolysis → FFA activate UCP1). Inhibited by: purine nucleotides (GDP, ADP). DNP (2,4-dinitrophenol): synthetic/toxic uncoupler — lipophilic weak acid → carries H+ across membrane. Once used as weight-loss drug (increases metabolic rate) but BANNED due to fatal hyperthermia, cataracts, agranulocytosis. Both → increased O2 consumption, increased substrate oxidation, decreased ATP, increased heat. Reference: Harper's — Uncouplers.

questions.questionText: An infant develops jaundice, hepatomegaly, and bleeding diathesis shortly after the introduction of fruit juices. Which of the following pathways is likely to be primarily affected during a normal physiological state in this patient?
questions.optionB: Cleavage of Fructose-1-phosphate to DHAP and Glyceraldehyde
questions.explanation: The child has Hereditary Fructose Intolerance (HFI), caused by a deficiency in Aldolase B. Adding fruit juices (sucrose/fructose) leads to accumulation of Fructose-1-phosphate, trapping intracellular inorganic phosphate. This impairs both glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis (leading to severe hypoglycemia) and causes ATP depletion, resulting in hepatomegaly, jaundice, and bleeding. Aldolase B normally cleaves Fructose-1-phosphate into Dihydroxyacetone phosphate (DHAP) and Glyceraldehyde.
